messages.author: jfbrennan
messages.body:
If the React team went with Option 2, then yay! and good on them for picking the right solution. 

I'm not seeing anything here that clarifies their choice though, so here's me BEGGING the React team to do Option 2.

There are a lot of assumptions and faulty logic in this thread (and some great ideas too), so I feel the need to speak up:
- Let's step back and take in the promise of Web Components. Web Components are supposed to enable custom standards-based framework-agnostic HTML elements, as such React cannot do ANYTHING that would dictate how a WC should have been developed. If it's a working WC on its own, then React must ensure it still works inside React as-is. React has to be the flexible and adaptable one, not the Web Component authors. In other words, React must not go anywhere near a solution that compels people to start creating React-compliant Web Components. History tells us this could easily become the norm if React screws up its implementation.
- React must not assume anything about how developers create WC. All that talk above assuming "most developers use Skate anyway so..." is a dangerous and naive assumption about what "most developers" are doing now and certainly what they'll do in the future.
- The dictating of what WC best-practices are as relating to attributes and properties, and therefore what React should base its implementation on, is also a dangerous and naive assumption. **There are perfectly valid cases for a WC to bind attributes and properties and perfectly valid cases NOT to.** That same web.dev article linked to above was written 6 years ago and gives contradicting advice about this subject. React cannot assume one or the other is the expected way or the preferred way and therefore a required WC implementation detail.
- HTML attributes only accept string values (`DOMString`s, but those are just strings). If developers don't know this it's because they've been living in Reactland for too long. So, it is perfectly reasonable to set attributes as strings and let the WC take it from there. Leave it to the WC author, don't inject React's preferences into what should be a framework-agnostic component. If there happens to be a property, well then that's the WC authors invitation to set that instead of the attribute.
- Server-rendered HTML with WC markup. Enough has been said about that. 
- WC markup parsed and styled _before_ the Custom Element definitions are loaded (if ever). React shouldn't blow up trying to set properties. Like the rest of the web it should make do with what is available because on the web something is better than nothing. Web must be fault-tolerant.
- Custom HTML tags (prefixed if you're playing it safe) along with custom attributes are perfectly valid HTML and can be styled with CSS. No Custom Elements definition at all. React must respect these and set their attributes. This has been part of HTML for decades and has worked with virtually every JS framework for just as long, and based on my experience is the absolute future of design systems (see [Mdash](http://m-docs.org) and the [TAC methodology](https://jordanbrennan.hashnode.dev/tac-a-new-css-methodology))

Again, if Option 2 was the chosen path then awesome! That is such a good signal from the React team. If not, React needs to reverse course.

messages.author: bahrus
messages.body:
@jfbrennan

Some edge cases to consider, and bringing you up to date on this tortuous debate:

1.  As pointed out by @josepharhar, if you name a property beginning with the word "on", React could confuse it with an event.
2. For years, some members of the React team claimed web components were fundamentally flawed, because they encouraged developers to define attribute names without dashes, which could conflict with future platform attributes, and that is why they were dragging their feet on supporting web components.  Yet by React opting for option 2 (yes, they did endorse option 2 many comments and months ago), they essentially shot their own argument in the foot (speaking of footguns) -- if a developer chooses to use dashes in their attribute names to address this very concern, say on the server side for setting initial values, they will seemingly run into trouble on the client side, if they utilize the same React template for both scenarios.
3. It's unclear how making all bindings work only with properties would make React work with solutions like [HTMX](https://github.com/bigskysoftware/htmx) or Alpine.js or other such solutions, at least if they need the values of those attributes to change dynamically.

At this point, I almost wonder if just leaving things the way they are wouldn't be better (at least compared to option 2).  It provides quite a testimony to React's engineering prowess, and/or their stance on issues [like these]( https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Vendor_lock-in).  

In the meantime, it has helped propel initiatives like the [custom element manifest](https://dev.to/thepassle/reactifying-custom-elements-using-a-custom-elements-manifest-2e), which I think also showcases the engineering prowess and commitment to the same vendor lock-in issue mentioned above, but within the web component community.

Most libraries, like [Shoelace](https://shoelace.style/frameworks/react) simply document separately how to integrate with React.  If I were a member of the React team, I would find this quite embarrassing, personally, so I've grown rather fond of seeing these "accommodations" for the special framework that is React.  (On the other hand, I'm not the one who is having to write such documentation, so my flippant attitude is probably not shared by anyone else, so, yes, by all means proceed with option 2 (sigh)).

messages.author: jfbrennan
messages.body: @bahrus thanks for those insights. I'm glad to see more people and projects holding React's feet to the fire when it comes to Web Components - it is indeed React that needs to figure out how to play nice with WC, not the other way around. My preference is actually Option 0 where React ultimately fails to ship a good WC solution so we can then justify rewrites with simple, standards-compliant frameworks and never ever try to outsmart the web platform again 😇

messages.author: jfbrennan
messages.body:
@msteller-connyun nothing really to do with React. The event names themselves - native and custom - don't include the "on" prefix. Shoelace event names, Mdash event names, etc. don't include "on" in their event names. The spec defines event handler _attribute names_ start with an "on" prefix _"followed by the name of the event"_. So, using whatever framework (or no framework) when binding events in the template you use the event name prefixed with the framework syntax:
```
Event name: "slblur"

HTML
<sl-button onslblur="...">

Riot (uses vanilla HTML syntax)
<sl-button onslblur="...">

Vue
<sl-button v-on:slblur="...">
<sl-button @slblur="...">

Svelte
<sl-button on:slblur="...">

React
<sl-button onslblur="...">
```

messages.author: WickyNilliams
messages.body:
React won't be changing the case at all, it'll use the event name verbatim. Since event names are case sensitive, and react can't know the casing of an event from a custom element, the only safe option is  to listen exactly to everything after `on`.

```
oneventName -> eventName
onEventName -> EventName 
onevent-name -> event-name
oneventname -> eventname
```

So in the case of the shoelace example it'll be `onsl-blur`

At least this was the case when I lasted tested the experimental build
